steps.dialogue: You've learned so much about [vocab]equal parts[/vocab]! Let's discover how these ideas connect to even bigger patterns in math.

Look at these two bars. [event:68ffd3420b1772b0] They're the same size, [event:68ffb2ed42dc8bdf]but [vocab]partitioned[/vocab] differently. [event:68ffb3573d87911e]  Think about why it is important that both bars are the same size. Imagine if one was longer.
Which shaded part is bigger? Click the bar with the bigger shaded part.

prompt.text: Click on the bar that has the bigger colored part.

step.dialogue: Look again. Check the size of each shaded part. When we split something into more parts, each part gets smaller.

[event:comparing_fraction_sizes_md] Let me help you see it: The top bar has 2 parts, the bottom has 4 parts. If we compare the size of a piece from the bottom bar to pieces in the top bar, you can see which one is bigger.

[event:comparing_fraction_sizes_md] Let me help you see it: The top bar has 2 parts, the bottom has 4 parts. If we compare the size of a piece from the bottom bar to pieces in the top bar, you can see which one is bigger.

steps.dialogue: [event:68ffbd4ccf3f9890]This is a big idea in math, when we split one whole into more [event:68ffbd7df231988e] parts, each part gets smaller. When you have fewer parts [event:68ffbd9ce4cf2546] each part is bigger.
See how the same whole can be partitioned different ways? The half is bigger than the fourth [event:68ffbe33f25125c3] because there are fewer parts to share the whole.
Three of these bars follow our rule about fractions. One breaks the rule. Click the bar that breaks our pattern.

prompt.text: Click the bar that doesn't belong.

step.dialogue: Once more. Let's try checking which bar has different-sized parts.
[event:690e5ae1fa5656fb]Let's check this bar together. [event:690e580adf6ecf48] Watch - when parts don't match like this, they're unequal. Select the bar with unequal parts.
[event:identifying_unequal_parts_hv] Let's solve this together. I'll check each: equal, equal, equal, NOT equal. This one doesn't belong.

on_correct.dialogue: Right. That one has unequal parts. You used the rule of equal parts to solve a puzzle!

steps.dialogue: Think about sharing things in real life. If these bars [event:6902862f16b21572] were chocolate bars to share with friends, which one gives each person the biggest piece?

prompt.text: Click on the bar where each person would get the biggest piece.

step.dialogue: Almost. Think about sharing - fewer people means bigger parts for each person.
[event:real_world_sharing_md] Let me help you compare. Watch the size of each piece - bigger pieces mean more chocolate for each friend. Which piece is largest?
[event:690e5b8fd450dac5] I'll help work this one out with you. Sharing with 3 people gives bigger pieces than sharing with 4 or 6 people.

on_correct.dialogue: You're using math to solve real problems. When we share with fewer people[event:6902869c1774e390], each person gets a bigger part.

steps.dialogue: Take a moment to think about how you figured that out. Did you compare the sizes [event:68ffbfa05b14dd8f] of the shaded parts, or did you think about sharing with friends?
Look at all these different fractions. They all follow one rule. What's the ONE thing that's always true about all of them?

prompt.text: Click on the answer that tells what's always true about fractions.

step.dialogue: Keep thinking about it. Look at all these bars - they have different numbers of parts, but what's the same?
[event:fraction_rule_discovery_md] Let's think about what we've been checking for in this lesson - what must be true about the parts for fractions to work? Look at every bar.
[event:fraction_rule_discovery_md] Let's think about what we've been checking for in this lesson - what must be true about the parts for fractions to work? Look at every bar.

on_correct.dialogue: Yes. Fractions are all about equal parts of a whole. That's what makes fractions work!

steps.dialogue: You've done great work! [event:68ffe256810c2806] You can recognize equal parts, [event:68ffe29216f92936] create them, compare them, [event:68ffe31be2af5f73] and even spot them in the real world. Next time, we'll use this understanding to name specific fractions [event:68ffe3f80ae72bff] and see how they relate to each other.
